entry.title: Are load balancers a single point of failure

link.title: Are load balancers a single point of failure

entry.content:
After following the tutorial for system design on Github (which is really great BTW), I found out about how load balancers and how they can be implemented by either expensive hardware load balancers or servers running HAProxy or Nginx. However, a question has been nagging me since I started studying it.

Who load balances the load balancer?

Hear me out, if the whole point of having multiple application servers is to provide high availability (HA) with a load balancer in front of it, and thus preventing a single point of failure, then won’t the load balancer itself be a single point of failure (SPOF)? Of course, the implicit assumption here is that the load balancer is less likely fail from load as it does less heavy lifting than the application servers, it just routes traffic! However, that does not mean it cannot fail due to stuff like hardware trouble, network trouble etc. etc.

The standard response to prevent load balancers becoming a SPOF is to have multiple load balancers in a HA cluster itself. But the problem is that the client will eventually need to point towards the healthy load balancer in case of a failure. How do we do that without now introducing a new SPOF?

What is the base case here? (At this point, developers should recognize a recursion when they see one). That’s right, DNS is actually the base case here! At the end of the day, clients connecting to servers will need to resolve the human-readable domain names to IP addresses using DNS and DNS can define multiple IPs belonging to 1 domain name. And there is no SPOF here as DNS is incredible resilient (the one connected to the internet, not those in private networks)

But wait, why do we even need load balancers when DNS itself can do the load balancing? Why create additional servers when the DNS can do it natively? I guess its to have more control over the connections and to implement routing logic. Software load balancers can do more than just routing, you can forward requests to servers by sessions, terminate SSL or scale out servers on the fly. For full benefits, you can refer to the Github link aboout system design above.

Besides using load balancers and DNS to achieve high HA, there is another way of doing it, which is to bundle your app with an SDK and write your routing logic within it. However, this method only works if your client is a mobile app or an internal application server. If your client is the browser, your best bet is still DNS + load servers.
entry.summary: After following the tutorial for system design on Github (which is really great BTW), I found out about how load balancers and how they can be implemented by either expensive hardware load balancers or servers running HAProxy or Nginx. However, a question has been nagging me since I started studying it.

entry.title: Inspecting streaming sites using Chrome web inspector

link.title: Inspecting streaming sites using Chrome web inspector

entry.content:
We have all done it at some point in our lives, where we watched online movies for free on websites like putlocker, 123movies etc. The basics of video streaming sites are shown in this article on zippia. As a recap, streaming sites often do not host their own content, but rather offer a search service to access these types of content on 3rd party hosts. The legality of these websites are grey but they still get served DMCA notices. Users seems to be off the hook if they do not download these movies or organize a big watch party. These websites have 2 types of ads: 1. banner ads, which are displayed on their websites and 2. video ads, which are embedded within the video itself.

However, I would like to investigate further, in particular the behaviour of these websites. They seem to have new tabs that opens on click and disable right click. So lets try to understand the more technical questions:

  1. How are these websites designed?
  2. What requests are the websites making?
  3. What are the risks of watching a video streamed this way?

How are these websites designed?

So lets imagine this scenario: you Googled a movie title, and a website offers to stream it for free. You enter the website and click on the video. A new tab opens, it links to a VPN site, you closed it. There is some buffering, and the video starts to play. I have tried out the steps above by Googling “Avengers 2012 watch free” and got into a streaming website.

HTML Elements

So first of all, we can do a simple inspect element, and we will find out that the video is embedded in an iframe. For example, a random monetized video from Youtube can be embedded with an iframe tag (this monetized part will be important later):

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/1dgJeXaxACM" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>

When you embed another website through an iFrame, it loads any HTML/CSS/JS from the embedded website within your website. It is similar to embedding a Youtube video. If you embed a video with ads from Youtube, it loads all of Youtube’s HTML/CSS/JS and the video’s ads will be displayed as well, as per Youtube’s policy. However, Youtube’s embedded video is definitely more benign, its just showing ads. The website that is embedded on streaming sites might be doing much more.

Scripts

So after looking at the HTML elements, lets look at the scripts. In this case, the scripts are minified so we are unable to understand them, but we can understand them through the behaviour. If the browser opens a new tab to another website (might even be legit websites) when you click on the video, it means that you have clicked on an ad. Most modern browsers nowadays block popups that are not requested, but in this case, you requested it ever since you click play on the movie, according to javascript.info

// popup blocked
window.open('https://javascript.info');

// popup allowed
button.onclick = () => {
  window.open('https://javascript.info');
};

Source: javascript.info

Sometimes they will even outright ban right click altogether on the video. For those websites, the aim is simple, don’t let your viewers/competitors download your souce by right clicking “save video as”. Youtube does this as well.

What requests are the websites making?

Using Google Chrome console, we can further see the requests that the website is making. There are 5 requests in this example, one of them an ad request from popcash.net. If I start my adblock extension on chrome, this popcash.net request will be blocked.

5 requests:

4 requests now after adblock is activated:

One thing to pay attention here is the picture with 5 requests. As you can see, the origin of this website is not the same one as I started but rather the embedded website, which means that the request is being made from the embedded site.

What are the risks of watching a video streamed this way?

According to cybersecurity experts, some malicious websites are able to harm your computer when you browse it by using exploit kits (but the articles are from a long time ago and they concern the use of Adobe flash player which is long known to be unsafe), so it is possible that your computer will be harmed. However, if those exploits still exists and not patched, it will be dangerous on any website, not just limited to illegal streaming sites. And we also know their motives, they are just like Youtube except they don’t honour copyrights. Given the possibility that any website might be harmful and we know their motive, t is up to you to figure out whether you want to take the risk.

Thank you for reading my amateur sleuthing, see you next time.
entry.summary: We have all done it at some point in our lives, where we watched online movies for free on websites like putlocker, 123movies etc. The basics of video streaming sites are shown in this article on zippia. As a recap, streaming sites often do not host their own content, but rather offer a search service to access these types of content on 3rd party hosts. The legality of these websites are grey but they still get served DMCA notices. Users seems to be off the hook if they do not download these movies or organize a big watch party. These websites have 2 types of ads: 1. banner ads, which are displayed on their websites and 2. video ads, which are embedded within the video itself.

entry.title: Number of games to reach legend in Hearthstone

link.title: Number of games to reach legend in Hearthstone

entry.content:
So last season, I managed to get Legend in Hearthstone. A combination of many things - an easier rank system, a new demon hunter class and the free time I’ve got due to the Covid-19 quarantine notice spurred me to touch Hearthstone again since I’ve quit playing it last year.

As I was grinding away to Legend, I found myself thinking - how many games do I need to get to Legend? It is not a straightforward probability question as if you lose you will undo your progress, so if you have a win rate of 60% you can’t just divide by 60 and multiply by 100. It is essentially a random walk.

Apparently if you Google this question, you will get to Primedope’s website. However, the simulator is buggy and won’t let me calculate the average number of games from Diamond 5 onwards, so I wrote my own.

I also added a simple random walk graph to show the progress from Bronze 10 to Legend and it confirms my suspicions that D5 to Legend is so much harder compared to the other ranks. It is almost like a negative exponential curve, slopes quickly in the beginning, but plateauing at the end when the win streak bonuses and the previous rank bonuses no longer apply.

I reached Legend in 148 games which I tracked from Diamond 10 onwards. A below average record according to my simulator. Ouch.

But hey! In the end, an achievement is still an achievement! (although I wasn’t particularly proud that I used the OP demon hunter towards the end) You can find the code here and add me on Battlenet: #Mthrandil1510
entry.summary: So last season, I managed to get Legend in Hearthstone. A combination of many things - an easier rank system, a new demon hunter class and the free time I’ve got due to the Covid-19 quarantine notice spurred me to touch Hearthstone again since I’ve quit playing it last year.

entry.title: Making a racing chart
entry.content:
I remember watching my first racing chart, which was about the values of companies, and I was like “hey, this shit is pretty cool.” I never really thought about doing it, until my friend introduced me to flourish which allows making racing charts in a painless way. Now, I am a programmer, and I know there are ways to do this without a ready made software like flourish charts, probably R’s GGPlot or JS’s DS3. Nevertheless, since I know flourish exists, I don’t have to Google on how to make a racing chart, the answer is already there. And since the answer is already there, I now thought of making a racing chart.

Ok, now that I have the tools, I needed an interesting dataset to work with. After scouring Youtube racing chart channels for ideas, I realized that the more popular topics has been done before (i.e. movies, countries by GDP, companies market cap etc.). Of all the topics, I realized nobody had worked on the dota dataset before, and so this will be my first test.

Opendota is a website that has an open API for us to get Dota2 data, and this will be our datasource. The main idea is to get the most picked heroes by professional players and see its rise and fall over time. I have made a Python notebook on my scrapers which I will release soon, but here are some of my thoughts about racing charts after trying it out.

Needs context

Taking a look at this racing chart made by another esports fan (in Unity no less!), we can see how his bar chart is obviously better. He provides context to the changes in the data by overlaying the tournaments going on in the top left corner. A CS:GO fan who takes a look at this will immediately able to connect the stats with the background information on the tournament. I find that racing charts really need some background context to explain the rise and fall of stats, else it is just a jumbled pile of stats.

Better to accumulate, unless the data is seasonal

Racing charts are used for racing things - imagine at a 400m race, you wait for everyone to arrive before you can start the next baton, wouldn’t that be weird? Similarly, we would want the stats to be cumulative, instead of resetting at every timestamp. It is just what racing charts are good at showing! However, some stats are just difficult to do cummulatively - take this Dota stats for example. Since we are taking professional data, there would be some weeks where there would be no competition and the hero use rate would be low. There is just no way to normalize between time periods and still do it cumulatively, so here we just reset it every week. It all depends on the context of the data and what are you trying to show. A changing meta? The most used hero of all time? Your choice.

Cheating with graphics

You have heard of lying with statistics, now have you heard of cheating with graphics? In a racing chart, there are plenty of things to tune - the speed of bar growing, the speed of bar overtaking, etc. These small tweaks have a big effect on the overall presentation. For example, if you set the speed to be too fast, the movements can be very chaotic, and the data might seemed more volatile than normal. If you set it to be slow, the data seems to be more stable. A big part of making this racing chart is the tuning of the parameters, not just to make it look nice, but to add to the “lie” that you are creating with the statistics.

Have you tried making racing charts? Do share some of your thoughts
entry.summary: I remember watching my first racing chart, which was about the values of companies, and I was like “hey, this shit is pretty cool.” I never really thought about doing it, until my friend introduced me to flourish which allows making racing charts in a painless way. Now, I am a programmer, and I know there are ways to do this without a ready made software like flourish charts, probably R’s GGPlot or JS’s DS3. Nevertheless, since I know flourish exists, I don’t have to Google on how to make a racing chart, the answer is already there. And since the answer is already there, I now thought of making a racing chart.

entry.title: Java Pass By Reference
entry.content:
I have always heard people mentioning about Java, and how Java Objects pass by reference, while Java primitives pass by value. I think it can be simply compared to mutable and immutable objects from Python. Consider the code snippet below:

//Deserialization bomb - deserializing this stream takes forever
static byte[] bomb() {
    Set<Object> root = new HashSet<>()
    Set<Object> s1 = root;
    Set<Object> s2 = new HashSet<>();
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        Set<Object> t1 = new HashSet<>();
        Set<Object> t2 = new HashSet<>();
        t1.add("foo"); // Make t1 unequal to t2
        s1.add(t1); s1.add(t2);
        s2.add(t1); s2.add(t2);
        s1 = t1;
        s2 = t2;
    }
    return serialize(root); // Method omitted for brevity
}

Source: Bloch, Effective Java, pg. 340

While Bloch is trying to teach us about the dangers of deserialization, what I saw was instead the way the variable root was changing. If one were to insert a print statement into the for loop, you would get the following printouts:

[[], [foo]]
[[[], [foo]], [[], foo, [foo]]]
[[[[], [foo]], [[], foo, [foo]]], [[[], [foo]], foo, [[], foo, [foo]]]]
[[[[[], [foo]], [[], foo, [foo]]], [[[], [foo]], foo, [[], foo, [foo]]]], [[[[], [foo]], [[], foo, [foo]]], foo, [[[], [foo]], foo, [[], foo, [foo]]]]]

But hey, how is root getting modified? didn’t we just add t1 and t2 to s1, and then we even set s1 to t1. Shouldn’t the reference to root be totally lost???

One of the revealing clues is that although root is changing, root always has 2 children. This means that the elements are added to root the first time, but it has changed. This tricky code is a combination of a cool recursive-like structure as well as the Java pass by reference. If we were to convert the code to Python and treat HashSet as a list, we can see the corresponding code as:

root = []
s1 = root
s2 = []
for i in range(100):
    t1 = []
    t2 = []
    t2.append("foo")
    s1.append(t1)
    s1.append(t2)
    s2.append(t1)
    s2.append(t2)
    s1 = t1
    s2 = t2

Seeing this code makes it clear. In the first iteration, root/s1 is a list of [] and [“foo”] which is t1 and t2 respectively, however, when you set s1=t1, the new s1 is the element inside root, the [], and when you append to it, you essentially modify it. Running the code will yield the same result as Java. This problem is tricky, but is not hard to understand once you write in out step by step and understand how Java passes by reference. After all, typesafe just means that you can’t easily convert an Object to another class, it does not mean that something is always invariant and immutable.

TIL:

  1. Java pass by reference works for Objects which is kinda like having a mutable object in Python. Similarly, Java pass by value is kinda like immutable objects
  2. Typesafe for Java only means safety for class conversion and not mutable objects.
entry.summary: I have always heard people mentioning about Java, and how Java Objects pass by reference, while Java primitives pass by value. I think it can be simply compared to mutable and immutable objects from Python. Consider the code snippet below:
